abstract: Communication history in socialist Europe has long been defined by its association with state propaganda. Following its inception, the USSR established itself as the world’s first “propaganda state”—a term coined by Peter Kenez to describe a radical new communications order that would eventually be emulated across the Eastern Bloc. In his seminal 1985 study, Kenez argued that the Bolshevik regime was unique not just for its ideological ambitions, but for its systematic attempt to utilize political education to forge a "new humanity" capable of inhabiting a transformed society. Under this framework, propaganda was not merely a tool for messaging but a foundational instrument of social engineering.
